chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame: stack shows Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum at top, followed by X.509 conversion functions, all within libUbikeyJni.so. No allocator/sanitizer frames are shown, so Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is the crashing function.

Given only symbol names and no code, infer likely behavior: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum presumably walks ASN.1 structures, computing child element counts or indices within a parsed certificate. Crashes in such routines commonly stem from trusting length/offset metadata from untrusted ASN.1 data, leading to out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer dereference.

Call chain within native: CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. CERT_Load suggests it takes raw certificate bytes and parses them to internal ASN.1 representation, then converts to X.509 objects. The top frame indicates the fault is deep in ASN.1 tree handling, not an artificial test stub.

JNI entry: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName is the bridge, used to obtain the subject name from a certificate. Realistic application behavior: the app likely loads user or server certificates from files, network, smart card, or OS keystore, then calls this JNI to parse them. In a banking app, certificates may come from external devices (UbiKey, smart card) and can be tampered with by a local attacker at least, possibly over network if server side also uses this library.

Fuzz harness: fuzz_one_input calls the same JNI bridge. The fuzzer almost certainly passes arbitrary or malformed certificate bytes, some of which cause the crash by triggering edge cases in the ASN.1 parser. This demonstrates that there exists at least one input blob that drives the parser to a faulty state. While fuzz input may be more extreme than production, ASN.1 parsers must be robust against arbitrary untrusted input, and malformed certificates are realistic (attacker can present a crafted cert/token).

Attacker control and validation: The crash is in ASN.1 tree traversal; these routines typically operate on structures derived directly from parsing untrusted certificate bytes. If there were solid bounds checks, fuzzing would tend to produce graceful parse failures rather than a native crash. The absence of any allocator/sanitizer frames and the fact the top frame is a custom ASN.1 helper strongly suggests a logic or memory safety bug (e.g., miscomputed child index, integer under/overflow in length, unchecked loop over child elements) rather than a clean error-path abort. Since we know this was caught by JNI fuzzing, the input is definitely attacker-controlled at the JNI boundary.

Exploitability: Crashes in ASN.1 child-count routines are classically exploitable (cf. numerous OpenSSL/ASN.1 CVEs) because they imply the code is using offsets/lengths from the untrusted structure to perform pointer arithmetic and memory accesses. Even if the current symptom is a crash (DoS), it plausibly can be groomed toward controlled memory corruption. Considering this is part of a security-sensitive banking library, attacker-controlled crafted cert data leading to native-level crash should be treated at least as high severity. Without instruction/heap/stack details, cannot assert RCE, but denial-of-service and potential memory corruption are realistic.

Environment/harness considerations: The presence of fuzz_one_input does not by itself make the bug non-real; it just proves the library misbehaves under malformed input. jGetSubjectName is a legitimate JNI API; CERT_Load and the X.509 conversion routines look like production code, not harness-only helpers. Nothing in the stack suggests use of dummy stubs or impossible parameters. Therefore, it is unlikely to be a harness-only artifact.

Given these factors, classify as a likely genuine vulnerability: attacker-controlled certificate/ASN.1 data flowing through jGetSubjectName into CERT_Load can trigger incorrect ASN.1 child computation in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, leading to a native crash and probably memory unsafety. Impact: at minimum a robust DoS of any feature relying on this JNI; at worst, potential memory corruption exploitable for code execution. With current information, rate severity as high and exploitability as theoretical/practical rather than confirmed RCE.
reasons: Crash occurs in custom ASN.1/X.509 parsing code (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) rather than in generic allocator or harness-only logic.
Input reaching this code is attacker-controlled certificate/ASN.1 data passed via JNI jGetSubjectName, as demonstrated by the fuzz harness.
ASN.1 child-count / traversal bugs are a well-known source of out-of-bounds access and exploitable memory corruption when lengths/indices from untrusted structures are not fully validated.
No evidence suggests the crash is caused by unrealistic JNI arguments or a special fuzz-only path; all native frames are from the production libUbikeyJni.so.
recommendations: Review and harden ASN.1 parsing and traversal routines, especially Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, ensuring all length, offset, and child index calculations are bounds-checked against the underlying buffer and structure sizes.
Add rigorous input validation in CERT_Load / Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* to reject malformed or inconsistent ASN.1 trees before deeper traversal, and ensure safe failure instead of native crashes.
Run targeted fuzzing of certificate/ASN.1 inputs against libUbikeyJni.so with ASan/UBSan enabled to pinpoint exact out-of-bounds or overflow operations and confirm fixes.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName accepts or indirectly uses attacker-influenced certificate/ASN.1 data (from user, device, or network).
The crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is due to incorrect trust in ASN.1 structural metadata (lengths/indices) rather than a deliberate abort in error handling.
No additional hardening (sandboxing, process isolation) mitigates the impact of a native crash in libUbikeyJni.so in the target app context.
limitations: No decompiled native or Java code was available, so the exact faulty instruction and memory access pattern are inferred from symbol names and call stack only.
The specific nature of the crash (e.g., null dereference vs. out-of-bounds write) is unknown, so exploitability beyond denial-of-service cannot be conclusively proven.
The precise way the application surfaces jGetSubjectName to external inputs (file paths, network payloads, smart card data) is not provided.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted or malformed ASN.1/X.509 certificate to code that calls UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process a certificate or token whose raw bytes are under attacker control (e.g., via imported certificate file, external security token, or MITM on a connection where certificates are parsed client-side).
The app uses UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName on untrusted or semi-trusted certificate inputs in its normal operation.
libUbikeyJni.so is loaded in the main app process and JNI calls run with typical app privileges.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an ASN.1/X.509 certificate with intentionally malformed child structures (e.g., inconsistent length/child count fields) aimed at hitting edge cases in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum.

Deliver this crafted certificate to the target app through a realistic channel (such as importing a certificate file or presenting a manipulated token or server certificate) so that the Java layer invokes UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName on it.
When CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* parse this malformed ASN.1, their internal representations trigger incorrect child computation in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum.
The function performs out-of-bounds or otherwise invalid memory access, causing a native crash (denial of service) and potentially corrupting adjacent memory if heap/stack layout allows.
Iteratively refine the crafted ASN.1 structure under a debugger/fuzzer to attempt steering the crash into controlled memory corruption for possible code execution.
poc_commands: # Interact with the app UI or API to import/use the certificate so that UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName is called on the crafted data.
exploit.notes: The exact Java method signature and how certificates are provided to jGetSubjectName are unknown; the PoC assumes a typical pattern where the app loads certificate bytes from a file or external token and then calls the JNI for subject extraction.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy, which is a libc allocator/utility; next frame Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so is the first app-level function. Tools are unavailable (404), so I must reason conceptually from the stack only.

Decode_DER_ASN1_ST suggests ASN.1 DER parsing of a certificate. memcpy crash implies an out-of-bounds read/write or overlapping copy within that decoder. The call stack shows certificate-loading path: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName -> CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> __memcpy.

The fuzzer harness calls the same public JNI function that the real app uses, which will receive attacker-controlled certificate/DER bytes in realistic contexts (e.g., from a smart card, NFC, network, or file). There is no indication that the fuzz harness uses impossible arguments like NULL-only pointers; instead, fuzzing CERT input is exactly what the real-world untrusted data is.

In normal designs, jGetSubjectName likely takes a byte[] or similar from Java and passes it straight into CERT_Load/Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, which then run ASN.1 decoding. The crash inside memcpy during ASN.1 decoding strongly indicates insufficient bounds checking over length fields or constructed element sizes derived from the untrusted DER stream.

Because the crash happens in libc __memcpy and not in an explicit abort, it is almost certainly a memory corruption (heap/stack OOB) caused by incorrect length/size computation. ASN.1 decoders are historically vulnerable if not carefully validated. The library is custom (libUbikeyJni.so) and likely implements its own parsing.

This is not a pure harness bug: the harness simply supplies arbitrary DER-like blobs via the public JNI, matching a realistic attacker scenario. There is no sign of protective validation elsewhere in the call chain (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT are native internal, CERT_Load is a loader, all inside same lib). Therefore untrusted certificate data can drive memcpy size/offsets.

Given the limited info, I cannot produce exact C code, but the structural evidence (untrusted ASN.1 input -> custom decoder -> memcpy crash) fits a classic CWE-787/119 case and is likely exploitable for memory corruption, at least to cause robust DoS and potentially more. Impact could be high, but without allocator/sanitizer logs I keep it at 'high' not 'critical'.

Call sequence from Java/JNI is clear: fuzz_one_input (fuzzer) substitutes for real Java caller(s) to UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName. In production, jGetSubjectName would be called from some app logic passing certificate/response bytes. Thus, exploit path is: craft malicious cert/DER blob, get app to call jGetSubjectName with it, trigger decoder bug.

Thus I classify as a genuine vulnerability (memory corruption) with high likelihood. I list CWE-787 / CWE-119. Exploitability: theoretical to practical; PoC is just making the app process a crafted certificate or identity blob. ADB PoC commands can only be generic because I lack the concrete Activity and argument wiring; I will note these assumptions explicitly.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from custom Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside libUbikeyJni.so, indicating out-of-bounds memory access during ASN.1 decoding.
Data path originates from JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName, which in normal use will process untrusted or semi-untrusted certificate/DER data.
Fuzz harness merely replaces Java callers but uses the same JNI entry, so the crashing path is realistically reachable with attacker-controlled input.
No evidence of prior bounds checking before Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses memcpy, consistent with classic ASN.1 length/size parsing vulnerabilities leading to memory corruption.
recommendations: Review and fix Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and related ASN.1 parsing to perform strict bounds checking on all length and offset fields before calling memcpy/memmove.
Add centralized input validation for certificate/DER blobs in CERT_Load/Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, rejecting malformed structures early.
Enable hardened builds with ASan/UBSan and run targeted ASN.1 fuzzing on libUbikeyJni.so to confirm and localize the exact out-of-bounds condition.
assumptions: JNI method jGetSubjectName accepts certificate or DER-encoded data that can be influenced by external/attacker sources.
The fuzzing input format is consistent with how the production app passes data into jGetSubjectName (e.g., byte array).
The crash in __memcpy is due to out-of-bounds access rather than a benign abort unrelated to input-controlled sizes.

exploit.trigger_method: Crafted DER/ASN.1 certificate or identity data processed via UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName
prerequisites: Ability to supply or influence certificate/DER data that the app processes (e.g., via smart card, NFC token, network protocol, or imported file).
Target device runs vulnerable version of libUbikeyJni.so without additional sandbox mitigations blocking process crashes.
Attacker can repeatedly trigger certificate parsing to fine-tune payloads.
exploit_pipeline: Prepare a malformed DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 structure with inconsistent length fields designed to cause over-read/over-write during memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
Deliver this crafted blob through the app’s normal channel that ultimately invokes UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName (e.g., causing the app to load/verify the attacker-controlled certificate).
When the app invokes jGetSubjectName, CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST will parse the blob and reach the unsafe memcpy.
The corrupted memcpy operation triggers memory corruption, leading to process crash (DoS) and potentially controllable overwrites depending on allocator and surrounding heap layout.
poc_commands: # Then, in the app UI or via automation, trigger the code path that loads the certificate and calls UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName
exploit.notes: Exact Activity, package name, and parameter wiring are unknown; replace <PACKAGE>/<ACTIVITY> with real identifiers once discovered via manifest or dynamic analysis.

chain_of_thought: Stack trace shows crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName -> fuzz_one_input. First app-level native frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load, both in libUbikeyJni.so per lib map.

Tried to follow mandatory MCP steps: opened libUbikeyJni.so successfully, but search_functions_by_name for Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load, JNI bridge all returned 404 errors from the MCP backend, so I cannot decompile or inspect their implementations. That blocks detailed backward data-flow analysis and argument tracing for memcpy.

Given typical patterns, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST suggests a DER ASN.1 decoder, and CERT_Load likely parses a certificate blob. Crashing inside __memcpy during DER decode often indicates out-of-bounds read/write or mis-sized copy based on embedded length fields in untrusted data. Fuzz harness calls fuzz_one_input, which likely feeds arbitrary data into the Java/JNI path without validation to stress the parser.

Without decompiled code, I cannot prove whether memcpy is directly using attacker-controlled length or whether there are bounds checks inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load. However, ASN.1/DER parsing is notoriously complex and many historical bugs come from trusting length fields. The presence of a dedicated Decode_DER_ASN1_ST function strongly suggests custom or third-party ASN.1 handling where length validation bugs are plausible.

The JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName implies it extracts the subject name from a certificate structure, likely by taking a byte[] (certificate) from Java and passing it through CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST down to memcpy. In production, that byte[] will come from network, files, or smartcard; i.e., attacker-influenced. Fuzz harness confirms that native accepts arbitrary input. No evidence of harness-only misuse like forcing NULL pointers or impossible ownership patterns; the crash is in a deep parsing routine, not obviously in glue code.

Because I lack code, I cannot identify the exact memcpy call site, its src/dst/len expressions, or show absence of bounds checking. So I cannot assign maximum confidence. But the combination of: (1) memcpy crash, (2) deep in DER ASN.1 decode, (3) triggered by arbitrary fuzzer input via JNI, (4) common class of memory-corruption bugs in such parsers, makes it reasonably likely this reflects a real memory-safety flaw, not a benign abort or harness artifact.

Impact: if length fields from the certificate bytes are not strictly validated, a crafted certificate could cause memcpy to copy more bytes than the destination buffer, leading to heap/stack overflow (CWE-787/788) and potential RCE or at least native DoS. Given limited evidence, I classify severity as high but keep confidence around 0.65 to reflect uncertainty about exact exploitability.

Call sequence reconstructed from stack: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName (JNI bridge) -> CERT_Load -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> __memcpy. JNI bridge and intermediate C functions are in libUbikeyJni.so per lib map. Java-side call graph is not provided beyond fuzz_one_input, so I assume typical pattern where fuzz harness directly calls bridge with arbitrary bytes.

Exploit sketch (hypothetical): attacker crafts X.509/DER blob with malicious length fields in the Subject or related sequence, delivers it via whatever API eventually invokes jGetSubjectName (e.g., loading a certificate from server or smartcard). Native CERT_Load passes embedded length to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST which computes copy size incorrectly and calls memcpy, overflowing a heap buffer. With grooming, this could be turned into code execution; at minimum, it is a native crash on untrusted input.
reasons: Crash is in __memcpy invoked from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST during certificate loading, indicating possible out-of-bounds memory copy.
Path is reachable from JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName, which in real use will process attacker-influenced certificate/DER data.
Fuzz harness triggers crash via arbitrary input, consistent with unsafe parsing of length fields in DER ASN.1 structures.
No evidence that this is a harness-only misuse (e.g., forced NULL) or a deliberate abort; it appears as uncontrolled memcpy in parsing logic.
recommendations: Manually decompile CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so to locate the failing memcpy call and verify how length and destination buffer sizes are computed.
Add strict bounds checking when parsing DER/ASN.1: validate all length fields against the remaining buffer and destination buffer size before calling memcpy or similar.
Enable hardened allocators and address sanitizers in a test build, and create focused fuzz harnesses for CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to reproduce and minimize the crashing input.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName accepts raw certificate/DER bytes influenced by external input (network, file, or hardware token).
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST contains at least one memcpy where the length is derived from parsed ASN.1 length fields.
The crash is due to an out-of-bounds access rather than an unrelated libc/internal fault.
limitations: Could not decompile or inspect CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST due to MCP backend 404 errors, so memcpy argument sources are inferred, not proven.
Java-side implementation of UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName and its callers was not inspected; exact input origin and validations are unknown.
No concrete crashing input or register/memory state at the point of crash is available, so precise nature (read vs write, heap vs stack) of corruption is unknown.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted DER/X.509 certificate blob to the code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to load or process an arbitrary certificate or DER-encoded structure.
JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName is reachable with untrusted data through normal app workflows.
No additional isolation (e.g., separate process sandbox) prevents impact of native memory corruption.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 structure with malformed or oversized length fields in the Subject or related sequence fields.
Deliver this blob to the application via the mechanism that ultimately invokes UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName (e.g., importing a certificate or connecting to a controlled TLS endpoint).
When the app processes the certificate, JNI passes the raw bytes to CERT_Load, which invokes Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses the attacker-controlled length to call memcpy without sufficient bounds checking, causing out-of-bounds memory write/read and crashing or corrupting memory.
Iteratively refine the malformed structure to achieve reliable crash and, with deeper analysis, potential control over overwritten data structures for code execution.
exploit.notes: Exact exploitation details (heap layout, overwritten targets) require decompiling CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and analyzing the specific memcpy call site and allocation patterns.
